Very complex but the demand exists, the games don’t yet

The Meta AI data center is being built in a heavy industrial zoned area. Closest community is 8-10km away. If even a fraction of these projects get approved Alberta's north will be booming once again!  
Its amazing how Doomberg predicted this late spring while Trudeau was still in power

content:
My sister works as corporate HR for Canada's largest construction company/ 14th in North America and they can't find enough workers in Alberta's north area currently!  
Everyone in the industry has seen it coming. No companies want to hire apprentices as they all want to run lean, at the same time in the next 5-10 year we will have the largest percentage of trades people leave the workforce through retirement.
(All you need to do is go on any industrial worksite and you can abviously see how many workers are nearing retirement. There are large amounts of workers that are 50-65, and 30-40 years old and any other age group has very few representation.)

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
Just looked this up to provide you some facts instead of my gut industry assessment - 

The Retirement Wave (100% True),,The "silver tsunami" is a verified data point, not an exaggeration.

The Attrition Rate: According to BuildForce Canada's Latest Industrial Outlook, Canada will lose over 270,000 experienced tradespeople to retirement over the next decade.

Alberta's Share: The Business Council of Alberta notes that roughly 20% to 40% of Alberta's current skilled workforce needs replacement in the next 5 to 10 years.

Current Impact: Recent Statistics Canada data shows a staggering 14.4% year-over-year drop in the eldest construction cohort, proving that age-related attrition is already pulling thousands of journeypersons out of the Alberta workforce

The Real Disconnect: Thousands of young Albertans are trying to get into the trades, enrolling at institutions like NAIT and SAIT. The issue is that they cannot find a first-year or second-year sponsor to get their blue book stamped.,,
Companies Wanting to Run Lean (100% True),,This is the core breakdown in the training lifecycle. Companies want to bypass the education phase but still reap the rewards of skilled labor.

The "Lean" Paradox: General contractors and modular yards are focused on immediate project margins. They want workers who can produce at a high level immediately.

The Cost of Training: Hiring a green first-year apprentice requires matching them with a highly paid journeyperson who must slow down production to teach them. To save on immediate labor burdens, companies are choosing to compete over a shrinking pool of seasoned workers.

Poaching Over Training: Instead of building a workforce pipeline, companies are paying premium shift differentials and travel allowances to poach existing journeypersons from other job sites.
